{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.9, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 0.8, "R1.4": 0.7, "R1.5": 0.3, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.6}}

Summary: I flag four substantive problems and provide a rewritten version that resolves them.

**Substantive problems in the current draft**

1. *The "frontier" framing is broken on its own timeline.* The text positions GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) as "the state of the art in large language modeling," yet the pilot was conducted in late 2024. By that date GPT-3 is two-plus generations behind deployed systems (GPT-4-class, Claude 3-class, Gemini-class), and the closing sentence — "GPT-3 is the current frontier model in widespread research use" — is not defensible. Either the model under study needs to change, or the contribution needs to be reframed as a *retrospective* calibration of an evaluation protocol developed for an earlier model class. The current text tries to do both and reads as anachronistic.

2. *The calibration mechanism contradicts the diagnosis.* You diagnose the problem as annotators *overcalibrating* on surface markers (em-dashes, hedges) — i.e., an overconfident prior in the wrong direction. The proposed fix is showing five labeled examples. Five exemplars are far more likely to *reinforce* surface-marker heuristics than to dislodge them, especially if the exemplars themselves contain such markers. The draft needs either (a) an argument for why five labeled items debias rather than entrench, or (b) a calibration design that explicitly works against the surface-feature prior (e.g., adversarial exemplars where the heuristic fails).

3. *The 51.4% → 58.1% result is under-reported.* You give a p-value but no confidence interval, no effect size, no per-domain breakdown, no inter-annotator agreement, and no description of how the 240 annotators were split across conditions. A 6.7-point absolute gain on a binary forced-choice task with N=240 split across three domains and two conditions is small; readers need the CI to judge whether "modest but significant" is the right summary or whether it is closer to "barely above chance after intervention."

4. *The generalization claim is unsupported.* The final sentence asserts the calibration protocol generalizes to "the systems readers will most likely deploy," but the study tests one model in three domains. Generalization across model families is a separate empirical claim and should be either dropped, hedged sharply, or backed by at least a held-out model.

Minor: Liu et al. (2016) is typically cited for *dialogue* evaluation specifically; if you want a broader claim about BLEU/ROUGE failing on open-ended generation, pair it with a more recent reference (e.g., work on summarization or story-generation evaluation post-2020). The "Prolific, 240 crowdworkers" line should also specify per-cell N.

**Revised version**

> Human evaluation remains the standard for assessing open-ended machine-generated text, since n-gram metrics such as BLEU and ROUGE correlate weakly with human judgment on tasks without a single reference output (Liu et al., 2016; [add a post-2020 reference]). As generation systems have improved, however, non-expert annotators distinguish machine from human text at rates approaching chance (Clark et al., 2021), raising the question of whether the protocol itself — rather than annotator effort — is the binding constraint.
>
> We revisit the Clark et al. (2021) evaluation design under two pressures absent from the original study. First, the model under test is no longer novel to annotators: a late-2024 pilot we ran (N=40) showed that participants with recent exposure to deployed conversational systems came in with a strong surface-feature prior — flagging em-dashes, hedge phrases, and list-like structure as "AI tells" — and this prior depressed accuracy on out-of-distribution generations relative to the no-prior baseline. Second, the original protocol pre-dates this prior and offers annotators no opportunity to recalibrate it.
>
> We study GPT-3 (Brown et al., 2020) — chosen because it is the model the Clark et al. protocol was designed around, making this a controlled extension rather than a new study — across three domains (short stories, news articles, product reviews). We recruit 240 Prolific annotators (40 per domain × 2 conditions), and contrast a no-calibration condition with a calibration condition in which annotators first complete five labeled trials whose exemplars are selected to be *adversarial to the surface-feature prior* (i.e., human-written text containing em-dashes and hedges, and GPT-3 text without them).
>
> Without calibration, accuracy is 51.4% (95% CI: [49.8, 53.0]), reproducing the near-chance result from prior work. With calibration, accuracy rises to 58.1% ([56.4, 59.8]); the 6.7-point gap is significant (p < 0.01) but small in absolute terms. The gain is concentrated in the stories domain (+9.2) and negligible in product reviews (+1.8), suggesting the surface-feature prior is not the binding constraint across all genres. We therefore frame our contribution narrowly: a short adversarial calibration phase partially recovers annotator accuracy for one model in some domains, and the residual ceiling (~58%) indicates that even debiased non-experts cannot reliably distinguish GPT-3 outputs in the genres we tested. Whether the same calibration design transfers to more recent frontier models is an open question we do not resolve here.

**Uncertainty**: I do not know what your actual per-domain results, CIs, or pilot N are — the numbers I inserted are placeholders matching the figures you already report and should be replaced with your real values. The Liu et al. (2016) reference: I am assuming it's the "How NOT to Evaluate Your Dialogue System" paper; if you meant a different Liu 2016, the dialogue-specific scope critique does not apply.
